text: liminality is indeed to replace the outside chaos with an order of the inside, having radicalized and brought to exhaustion doubt and scepticism through personal and collective reflexivity. In the classical ritual passages to manhood one of the most common exercises is to force the initiands to cut off the sensory apparatus from the ‘world’ in order to gain an inward vision (often received in a dream-like state), a vision of truth that the initiand must elaborate and make manifest in the shaping of his new personality. As described by van Gennep (1960) and Victor Turner (1969), during ritual passages, the initiands or the neophytes are brought to question most radically their own sensory apparatus, just as they challenge social order by setting themselves apart from any normally accepted social rules from their betwixt and between position; they become nameless, timeless and socially unstructured, existing in a floating state of being, even as they acquire throughout the liminal period the necessary knowledge and experience in order that their transformed beings may eventually re-enter society and take up their new roles. It is by no means a coincidence that the driving principles in Hobbes’ and Descartes’ systems of thought relate back to two fundamental human experiences in liminality: fear (of violent death) and doubt (how can I know anything? What is my role in the production of truth?). The perspective adopted here is not entirely new; rather, the anthropological approach proposed speaks in evident ways to existing frameworks of analysis in political theory. The eschatological and ultimately millenarian structure of Hobbes’ political thought was, among others, emphasized in the analysis offered by J. G. A. Pocock. As argued by Pocock, Hobbes’ most radical innovation was to employ (and not delete) sacred history as a new instrument of secular power (Pocock 1989: 179; see also Martinich 1992). Methodologically, Pocock sought to establish a new type of history of political thought in which the available language and what could be termed the ‘taken-for-granted’ universe of the political thinker function as the basis of the textual interpretation. Pocock used as a theoretical basis the notion of paradigms, taking his cue from Thomas Kuhn’s The Structure of Scientific Revolutions. Pocock argued that political discourse has to be analysed by considering the paradigms within which a thinker poses problems and offers solutions (following here Kuhn, who said that the larger framework of scientific questioning was only very rarely put into question, and that ‘normal science’ was mostly a practice of puzzle-solving). By implication, Pocock argued that we have to analyse Hobbes by taking seriously the ‘intellectual paradigms’ within which Liminality in the Transition to Modernity 119 he lived and wrote (such as the common law tradition). This type of historical method was further developed by Quentin Skinner, and has indeed become an established tradition. However, to insist on ‘context’ never really answers what constitutes the context for the thinker himself, and in particular offers little aid in understanding thought at moments in which ‘paradigms’ (the available language and generally accepted ideas) are the very issue at stake. 3 With the concept of liminality, Pocock’s approach can be expanded to embrace not only language and intellectual paradigms, but also the wider field of human experience. This was exactly what was suggested by the political theorist, Eric Voegelin. Voegelin recognized that political thought had to be understood as symbolizations of real human experiences (see Voegelin 1974, 1990). Methodologically, Voegelin also recognized that thoughts are not simply second-order reflections of ‘reality’, but are themselves part of a historical process. Indeed, while Weber wanted to identify the ‘spirit of capitalism’, Voegelin tried to do the same, searching for the ‘moving forces’ in politics. Voegelin’s analysis of modern politics partly came to rest on his concept of ‘intramundane eschatology’, which has a strong affinity with Weber’s notion of ‘this-wordly asceticism’: in both cases a religious experience is immanentized as it comes to direct this-worldly action. It is with this approach in mind that the general life experiences during the crucial period in the development of the modern state, the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries, need to be considered as the conditions of the emergence of the model of ‘absolutism’. Put simply, the crisis to which ‘absolutism’ became a response was first and foremost a religious crisis, and it touched upon the most basic relations between man and God, society and nature. While Pocock was certainly right that any interpretation of concepts must relate to their con- and inter-textual use, it can furthermore be suggested that more than setting ideas against established paradigms, the Hobbesian and Cartesian systems of thought must be placed at precisely the point at which paradigms are collapsing, and new ones are in the making. Therefore, understanding ‘absolutism’ is not possible only by looking historically and substantially at what it offered in terms of territorial, politico- administrative arrangements. Absolutism was a solution to a specific problem. Understanding the breakthrough of absolutism in the seventeenth century involves a look at the terms in which the problem was posed as a problem, and at the long- term consequences of these formulations. It is in precisely this genealogical sense that Hobbes and Descartes are founding figures: while their solutions quickly became objects of dispute, it was their problematization and their framing of possible answers which would remain the hidden foundations of the modernity they helped to install. 3 In a very real sense both Descartes and Hobbes were engaged in devising a new language departing from (and never moving beyond!) clearly defined concepts and ideas. Descartes even flirted with the idea of creating an artificial language (see 1629 letter to Mersenne, quoted in Gaukroger 1995: 201).
